coredata.dc:title: Study on the real-time variation laws and mechanism of oil sample viscosity during ultrasonic irradiation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Jinbiao
Shen, Xiaozhuo
Mo, Xiaohai
Wu, Pengfei
Li, Chao
Lin, Weijun
Wang, Xiuming
coredata.dc:description: It is rather significant to reveal the real-time variation of oil sample viscosity during ultrasonic irradiation to research the mechanism of viscosity change. In this paper, we first simulate the acoustic field distribution law in the reaction chamber by using the finite element method and orthogonal experiment method, then measure the viscosity of the oil sample with temperature by vibration type viscometer and get the corresponding function equation by fitting. On this basis, we measure the viscosity of the oil sample with ultrasonic irradiation time and electric power change in real-time and in situ, and finally analyze the mechanism of oil sample viscosity variation by using a temperature recorder and cavitation noise method. The results show that the greatest influence on the acoustic pressure in the reaction chamber is the change of the transducer probe in the height Z direction, followed by the width X direction and the depth Y direction. The viscosity of the oil sample shows an exponential decay with the increase in temperature. With the increase of ultrasonic irradiation time and electric power, the viscosity of the oil sample is gradually reduced. By comparing the effect of heating and ultrasonic irradiation on viscosity, it is found that ultrasonic irradiation not only changes the viscosity through thermal effect but also the cavitation noise analysis and the phenomena observed in the experiment confirm that the cavitation effect and mechanical effect exist all the time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Real-time measurements
Viscosity variation mechanism
Acoustic cavitation
Finite element simulation
Orthogonal experiment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Influence of ultrasonic irradiation on the viscosity of oil sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11904384) and the Key Research Program of Frontier Sciences, CAS (No. ZDBS-LY-SLH037). Thanks go to Dr. Feida Cai (Institute of Acoustics, CAS) for the inspiring discussion about the experimental scheme, and Bin Xu senior engineer (Beijing Chang Cheng Aeronautical Measurement and Control Technology Co. Ltd), and Yulin Gong (Hydramotion, Ltd) for help in using the instrument. In addition, I would like to express my gratitude to Dr. Mi Zhang (Chongqing University of Science and Technology), Guanwen Sun, and Qin Chang for their assistance in the simulation, experiment, and data processing. Also, thanks go to my classmate Yuchen Zang for help with the English corrections.

This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11904384) and the Key Research Program of Frontier Sciences, CAS (No. ZDBS-LY-SLH037). Thanks go to Dr. Feida Cai (Institute of Acoustics, CAS) for the inspiring discussion about the experimental scheme, and Bin Xu senior engineer (Beijing Chang Cheng Aeronautical Measurement and Control Technology Co., Ltd), and Yulin Gong (Hydramotion, Ltd) for help in using the instrument. In addition, I would like to express my gratitude to Dr. Mi Zhang (Chongqing University of Science and Technology), Guanwen Sun, and Qin Chang for their assistance in the simulation, experiment, and data processing. Also, thanks go to my classmate Yuchen Zang for help with the English corrections.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Rheological characteristics of oil sample at different temperatures.
Results of sound field distribution for Experiment 8.
The viscosity of the oil varies with temperature.
The change of viscosity and temperature of the oil sample with ultrasonic irradiation time when the transducer input power is 50 W.
The change of viscosity and temperature of the oil sample with ultrasonic irradiation time when the transducer input power is 100 W.
The change of viscosity and temperature of the oil sample with ultrasonic irradiation time when the transducer input power is 150 W.
Time domain signal within ultrasonic irradiation 5 mins at 50 W.
Frequency domain signal within ultrasonic irradiation 5 mins at electrical power of 50 W.
The integral area (i.e., cavitation intensity) changes with the increase of ultrasonic irradiation time under different electric power conditions.
The factors and their levels that were studied by the orthogonal method.
The analysis table of experimental results.

head.ce:title: Study on the real-time variation laws and mechanism of oil sample viscosity during ultrasonic irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Acoustics, Institute of Acoustics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Acoustics, Institute of Acoustics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Engineering Research Center of Sea Deep Drilling and Exploration, Beijing 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Engineering Research Center of Sea Deep Drilling and Exploration

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Engineering Research Center of Sea Deep Drilling and Exploration, Beijing 100190, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Acoustics, Institute of Acoustics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is rather significant to reveal the real-time variation of oil sample viscosity during ultrasonic irradiation to research the mechanism of viscosity change. In this paper, we first simulate the acoustic field distribution law in the reaction chamber by using the finite element method and orthogonal experiment method, then measure the viscosity of the oil sample with temperature by vibration type viscometer and get the corresponding function equation by fitting. On this basis, we measure the viscosity of the oil sample with ultrasonic irradiation time and electric power change in real-time and in situ, and finally analyze the mechanism of oil sample viscosity variation by using a temperature recorder and cavitation noise method. The results show that the greatest influence on the acoustic pressure in the reaction chamber is the change of the transducer probe in the height Z direction, followed by the width X direction and the depth Y direction. The viscosity of the oil sample shows an exponential decay with the increase in temperature. With the increase of ultrasonic irradiation time and electric power, the viscosity of the oil sample is gradually reduced. By comparing the effect of heating and ultrasonic irradiation on viscosity, it is found that ultrasonic irradiation not only changes the viscosity through thermal effect but also the cavitation noise analysis and the phenomena observed in the experiment confirm that the cavitation effect and mechanical effect exist all the time.

ce:section.ce:para: To date, oil is still one of the most direct energy supply substances, while the extraction and effective utilization of heavy oil with enormous reserves is still a pressing problem to be solved. Countries with abundant heavy oil resources [1,2] mainly include Venezuela, Canada, the United States, China, etc. China's heavy oil is mainly distributed in the oil fields of Liaohe, Xinjiang, Shengli, Bohai, etc. One of the main factors why it is difficult to extract heavy oil is its high viscosity which results in poor flowability [3].

The viscosity of heavy oil is a greatly complex physical quantity that is affected by many factors and generally decreases with the increase in temperature, called the viscosity-temperature properties of the fluid. Ultrasonic technology could also change the viscosity of heavy oil, but the mechanism of viscosity change has not been determined so far. Most scholars [4–6] at home and abroad have studied the changes in chemical structure, microscopic morphology, viscosity, and freezing point of heavy oil after the ultrasound to analyze the mechanism of viscosity change of heavy oil caused by ultrasound. Considering that the viscosity of heavy oil is not constant during the ultrasound, the real-time characterization of the viscosity change of oil sample during the ultrasound is also essential for the study of the mechanism.

The commonly used methods for measuring viscosity include the rotational method and the falling ball method, but these methods are relatively traditional and unable to measure the variations in the viscosity of oil samples in the experimental setup in situ and in real-time. Emerging viscosity measurement methods include ultrasonic and vibration methods, which can be used to measure the viscosity change of oil samples in situ and in real time [7–9], and in recent years, several scholars have also conducted research on liquid viscosity measurement using emerging methods.

As early as 1949, Mason et al. [10] proposed the inversion of viscosity using the shear wave reflection method. Later Richard [11] clarified the relationship between the relevant parameters of ultrasound and the kinematic viscosity of the liquid. Hunston [12] in 1971 proposed the use of a strip waveguide immersed in a liquid to measure the shear properties of the liquid, using the change in wave attenuation measured under air and liquid conditions to obtain the viscosity, which could be measured for samples with viscosities as low as 3.6 mPa·s. Next researchers [13] at the University of Chicago invented in 1997 an ultrasonic viscometer for measuring the viscosity of liquids, which was based on the relationship between the reflection coefficient of the transverse wave and the viscosity to obtain the viscosity. At the end of the 20th century, Matsiev proposed a method to measure fluid viscosity and density using the variation of bending resonator impedance [14].

At the beginning of this century, the shear wave method was used by many scholars for viscosity measurement of glycerol [9], edible oil [15,16], lubricating oil [17], asphalt [18,19], polymer resin [20], polymer film [21], slurry [22], honey [23], etc. However, this method is affected by the performance of the transducer in actual measurements, which leads to unacceptable viscosity measurement errors. In contrast, the technique of inversion of viscosity by vibration method is more mature, such as quartz or metal tuning fork to measure the viscosity aspect of the liquid [24–27], the use of mechanical resonance method to make the sensor to measure the viscosity, density aspect [28–30].

Although there is a lot of researches regarding emerging viscosity measurement methods, in heavy oil research, researchers are mostly based on static or variable temperature viscosity measurement experiments, with few of them characterizing the dynamic process of sample viscosity in real-time, such as the real-time variation of heavy oil viscosity during ultrasonic irradiation has seldom been reported so far. However, heavy oil is a non-Newtonian fluid with strong non-homogeneity, and the viscosity of different layers will be different, and most of the heavy oil is a non-transparent liquid, so it is not easy to observe the experimental phenomenon during the ultrasonic irradiation. Therefore, in this work, we will choose a simple oil sample, and then use the portable viscometer based on the vibration method to make real-time in-situ measurements of the viscosity of the oil sample during the ultrasonic irradiation, and finally analyze the mechanism of the viscosity variation of the oil sample through temperature change and cavitation noise method, to provide a reference for the viscosity variation and mechanism study of heavy oil during the ultrasonic irradiation.
Based on the software of COMSOL Multiphysics finite element analysis, the simulation is performed using the acoustic-piezoelectric interaction module, whose main procedures are listed as follows:
(1) Geometric modeling

The related parts of the transducer consist of the back cover plate, a piezoelectric ceramic piece, a front cover plate, and a variation rod. The reaction chamber is a rectangular test chamber with length, width, and height of 200, 60, and 80 mm, respectively, customized for the experimental needs. And its material is polycarbonate (PC) with a thickness of 5 mm, which could resist high temperatures of 100 °C. The reaction chamber is colorless and transparent to facilitate the observation of the experimental phenomena. The final geometric model is shown in the figure below.
(2) Material properties and physical field settings

The structural steel (AISI 4340) with high strength and toughness was selected for the back cover, and its density, Young's modulus, and Poisson's ratio were set to 7910 kg/m3, 1.96*1011 Pa, and 0.30 respectively. Lead zirconate titanate (PZT-8) was selected as the piezoelectric ceramic material with a density of 7600 kg/m3. The front cover and the variator rod are made of hard aluminum with a density, Young's modulus, and Poisson's ratio of 2790 kg/m3, 7.15*1010 Pa, and 0.34.

The liquid in the reaction chamber occupies 3/4 of the volume (the liquid is oil, which will be introduced in detail in subsection 2.2.1), and the rest space is filled with air. The speed of sound of the liquid is set to 1530 m/s during the simulation, and all other parameters are set according to the parameters in the software material library.

Besides, the physical field of pressure acoustic is set in the reaction cavity domain, the liquid–air contact interface is set as the free boundary, and the outer wall of the reaction cavity is the hard acoustic field boundary. The overall performance of the transducer is reacted by the solid mechanic's physical field, and the electrostatic force on the upper and lower surfaces of the piezoelectric sheet is grounded and spaced out.
(3) Grid division and results calculation
The mesh is set as a free tetrahedral mesh, and its size is predefined to be refined, in which the mesh of the fluid contact part between the transducer and the reaction chamber will be more refined.

The calculation range of the frequency domain is set to 5–35 kHz with a step size of 0.01 kHz, and 400 characteristic frequencies are calculated. Finally, the sound field distribution in the reaction cavity at different frequencies and the vibration mode of the transducer at different resonant frequencies are obtained. Hereafter the sound field distribution patterns and absolute sound pressure amplitudes at different positions are investigated at second-order resonant frequencies, and the optimal results are obtained through orthogonal experimental analysis.

The placement and position of the transducer probe in the three-dimensional space of the reaction cavity will have an effect on the sound field distribution pattern and sound pressure magnitude, but if each condition is examined, the time is extremely long and the workload is enormous. Therefore, we can optimize the design of the experimental scheme through the orthogonal experiment method.

The orthogonal experiment [31,32] is an experimental design method in which some representative combinations are selected from all experiments, and the results of some experiments are analyzed to obtain an overview of the whole and to find the optimal level of combinations. The orthogonal experiment method has a balanced dispersion of experimental points, and the experimental data are comprehensive and comparable, which can not only compare the primary and secondary relationships between various factors, but also get the best combination of factors. Due to these benefits, it is widely used in all kinds of complex experiments.

Combined with the actual situation of the experiment, the main consideration of the transducer in the reaction cavity width (X direction), depth (Y direction), height (Z direction) of the three directions of the position change on the sound field, the three factors were selected for three different levels, the design of the factor level table is shown in Table 1 .

As shown in Fig. 1 , the X-direction refers to the distance between the position of the transducer probe and the left wall of the reaction chamber, the Y-direction indicates the distance between the probe and the front wall of the reaction chamber, and the Z-direction shows the distance between the probe and the bottom of the reaction chamber. Since other instruments need to be placed on the right half of the reaction chamber, so we only consider the change of the position of the transducer probe in the left half volume of the reaction chamber.

To increase the reliability of the experiments, we set up the blank columns (also called error columns), which can be used to examine the interactions between factors and the errors caused by other unexamined factors.

The factors were arranged on the L9 (33) orthogonal table, and the absolute sound pressure magnitude was used as an index to evaluate the change in sound field intensity. The two main methods of data analysis for simulation results are range analysis and analysis of variance (ANOVA).

Intuitive analysis method, that is, the extreme difference analysis method, by comparing the size of the extreme difference of each factor at different levels, the degree of influence of each factor is ranked and then gets the best sound field location, but this method cannot determine the significant degree of influence of each factor on the sound pressure, so it is required to be evaluated according to the results of the variance analysis. The variance analysis is aimed at determining the significance of the effect of each influencing factor on sound pressure using the F-test method in statistics, and these two analysis methods can be verified against each other to obtain more accurate data processing results.

In this paper, the portable viscometer (Hydramotion VL7-100B-d21) from Emerson (UK) is used in the experiment. The portable viscometer can respond to the viscosity change of oil sample in situ and in real-time, and the main measurement principle is to use the different viscosity of the oil sample to produce different viscous hindrance to the vibration of the instrument and invert the viscosity by the different degree of energy decay.

In addition, the viscosity of the oil sample was measured with temperature using a constant temperature water bath (BROOKFIELD TC-550); the temperature of the oil sample was collected in real-time during the ultrasonic irradiation using a temperature recorder (Lian Yi SH-X). The ultrasonic emission system used in the experiment includes an ultrasonic transducer (Institute of Acoustics, Chinese Academy of Sciences), a signal generator (Tektronix AFG3022C), a power amplifier (T&C Power Conversion, Inc. AG 1016), and a matching box (T&C Power Conversion, Inc. T1K-7A). The equipment used to collect the cavitation noise signal includes an oscilloscope (ROHDE and SCHWARZ RTM3004), and a hydrophone (PRECISION ACOUSTICS DCPS0273). The device for holding the liquid is a rectangular test chamber (Shenzhen Jingchengda Plastic Co., Ltd.) with length, width, and height of 200, 60, and 80 mm, respectively, and customized for the experimental needs.

The oil sample used in this paper was a viscosity homogeneous motor oil (Shell RIMULA 20W-50), and the rheological characteristic curves of the oil were measured using a rheometer (BROOKFIELD RV DV-III, rotor No. 21), and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . The blue circles and red triangles show the relationship between shear stress (left axis) and viscosity (right axis) with shear rate. The shear stress at different temperatures all changes linearly with the increase of shear rate, while the viscosity remains basically the same at this time, indicating that the oil is a Newtonian fluid.

Because motor oil is a post-processed material of petroleum, it has a certain similarity with heavy oil in composition. Standard motor oil has uniform viscosity and simple composition, and it is a translucent Newtonian fluid, so it is simple to observe experimental phenomena, so we can use motor oil to carry out experiments, and then extend the relevant technical means and methods to heavy oil at a later stage, which is more beneficial to scientific research.
(1) Measurement of oil sample viscosity-temperature curve

The flow chart of the experiment is shown in Fig. 3 . Firstly, we fill the reaction chamber with about 800 ml of oil sample, place it in a constant temperature water bath, and control the temperature of the oil sample from 20 to 90 °C, with each temperature increase interval of 5 °C. Then, the portable viscometer probe is completely submerged in the oil sample, and the temperature inside the oil sample is recorded by the temperature recorder. When the temperature is stabilized at the required measurement temperature, the viscosity data is recorded, and the viscosity under each temperature point is repeatedly measured five times to ensure adequate data accuracy, and finally, the viscosity value under each temperature point is obtained by data fitting.
(2) Real-time measurement of viscosity during ultrasonic irradiation

Connect the signal generator, power amplifier, matching box, and transducer in order, then use the signal generator to input 800 mV sine signal to the transducer, next constantly adjust the frequency button to make it match with the resonant frequency of the transducer (about 20.7 kHz).

In order to verify the accuracy of the variation law of viscosity at different times of ultrasonic irradiation, we chose three experimental conditions of electrical power (50, 100, and 150 W) to repeat the experimental law. The ultrasonic irradiation time is 30 min, and the action mode is continuous action. The viscosity of the oil sample was recorded by a portable viscometer at 30 s intervals during the ultrasonic irradiation, and 60 sets of data were recorded at each power.
(3) Real-time measurement of temperature and cavitation noise signals

The thermocouple probe of the temperature recorder, the probe of the hydrophone, and the probe of the portable viscometer are placed at the same position as the oil sample, and the measurement of the viscosity, temperature change, and noise signal of the oil sample can be considered as a single point measurement so that the influence of other factors can be avoided as much as possible.

The temperature logger can record one temperature point per second so that 1800 sets of temperature data can be collected, but only 60 temperature points are displayed in the results, which are intended to correspond to viscosity changes. The sampling frequency of the oscilloscope is 1.67 MHz, the cavitation noise signal is recorded every 30 s, and a total of 60 sets of data are recorded.

Through the temperature change during the ultrasonic irradiation and the regression equation fitted by the experimental procedure (1), the theoretical viscosity value corresponding to the oil sample when heated only can be obtained and compared with the viscosity affected by ultrasound recorded by the portable viscometer, which can be used to analyze whether the ultrasonic irradiation affects the viscosity through temperature only. In addition, the cavitation noise signal can be used to determine whether there is a cavitation effect during the ultrasonic irradiation and how strong the cavitation is, based on which the viscosity change mechanism of the oil sample can be studied as an aid [33,34].

We first evaluated the degree of influence of different factors on the size of the sound field by the polar difference R. The larger the polar difference R, the greater the influence of the factor on the sound field. The value of K in Table 2 is the average value of the summed sound pressure of three sets of experiments at one level corresponding to each factor, and the polar difference
R
=
K m a x - K m i n
(corresponding column).

It can be seen that R(Z) > R(X) > R(Y) from the table, so the degree of influence on the sound pressure is ranked as a height Z direction, width X direction, and depth Y direction. By comparing the magnitude of K values at different levels under the same factor, the optimal sound field conditions for the reaction cavity used in this paper can be obtained as follows: 25 mm in the X direction, 15 mm in the Y direction, and 45 mm in the Z direction, when the absolute sound pressure amplitude is 1590 Pa, which is greater than the dependent variable values of the other eight groups of experiments, and the results are reliable.

The effects of different factors on the magnitude of sound pressure are investigated by ANOVA to see if they are significant, and the results obtained by range analysis are verified with each other. The ANOVA results are shown in Table 3 , and the sum of squared deviations
S
S j
is calculated as:
S
S j
=
1 r
∑
i = 1
m
(
K
ij
2
-
T 2
/ n )
where, r is the number of levels, m generally taken as 3,
K
ij
is the value used to calculate the range, T is the sum of the values of the dependent variable for each group of experiments, and n is the total number of experiments.

In addition, the value of degrees of freedom is equal to the number of levels of each factor minus one. The ratio of the sum of squared deviations to the degrees of freedom is the variance. The ratio of the variance of each factor to the corrected error e is the F-value, which is the statistic of the F-test. Also, the F-value is compared with the theoretical F-test critical value table to obtain the significance of each factor's effect on the dependent variable.

Considering that
F X
= 2.377
,
F Z
= 4.059
, both are between 2 and 4.325, the X-direction and Z-direction have a certain degree of influence on the amplitude of the sound pressure. And the value
F Y
is too small and is considered an error treatment. Because
F Z
>
F X
the degree of influence of the three factors on the dependent variable is ranked as Z-direction > X-direction > Y-direction, which is exactly the same as the result of the range analysis.

Although the sound pressure amplitude is the largest under the conditions of Experiment 1, the condition is somewhat close to the left wall of the reaction chamber, which is not conducive to the overall uniformity of the liquid subjected to ultrasound. The sound pressure amplitude of Experiment 8 is similar to that of Experiment 1, the sound pressure is also uniformly distributed in the reaction chamber (Fig. 4 ), and the location is located in a more central position. Therefore, the condition based on Experiment 8 is chosen to carry out the later experimental studies.

Fig. 4(a) and (b) respectively show the distribution of absolute sound pressure in the two-dimensional section and three-dimensional whole of the reaction chamber when the transducer probe is 75 mm from the left side of the reaction chamber, 30 mm from the front side and 45 mm from the bottom. Such distribution results of the sound field are similar to the vibration modal results of the reaction chamber itself. After a systematic analysis, such sound field distribution results should be caused by resonance between the resonant frequency of the transducer (20.7 kHz) and the resonant frequency of the reaction cavity (20.598 kHz). The standing wave field formed in this state allows the local acoustic pressure in the reaction cavity to reach a very strong extreme point, thus creating a stronger radiation effect on the liquid.

The variation of oil sample viscosity with temperature measured by the portable viscometer is shown in Fig. 5 , in which the blue sphere indicates the actual measured data, the red dashed line indicates the result of fitting based on the actual measured data, and the model used for fitting and other related information is shown in the upper right corner of the figure.

The actual measurement data in the figure is the average result obtained by five measurements, and the standard deviation is also calculated at the same time. As can be seen from the figure, the error of the five measurements is minor, so the measurement results of the portable viscometer can be considered accurate. Because the actual viscosity-temperature scattering data of the oil sample used in this paper is in the form of exponential decay, the fitting model chosen is an exponential function, and the basic equation of its first-order function is:
y =
A 1
∗
e
( - x /
t 1
)
+
y 0
where
A 1
,
t 1
,
y 0
denote the declination angle, decay constant, and Y offset, respectively, and they are calculated from the actual data. The specific choice of several orders of function for fitting is assessed by the judgment coefficient R2, and the closer R2 is to 1, the better the fitting result. We fitted the first-, second-, and third-order functions to the actual data, and the R2 values obtained for the different functions were 0.99921, 0.99926, and 0.99926, correspondingly. Even though the R2 obtained by fitting the second and third-order functions is larger, the number of iterations of these two fits is greater which is overfitting, so the model chosen in this section is the first-order exponential function fitting model. Gradually increasing the number of iterations until the fitted equations converge and meet the regression accuracy requirements, the first-order exponential function model is iterated for a total of four times to achieve the convergence effect, so the final determination of the three constant term coefficients is:
y 0
= 17.60988 ± 1.04159
A 1
= 803.00704 ± 23.17187
t 1
= 20.32496 ± 0.38763
From this expression, the theoretical viscosity values corresponding to the different temperatures measured experimentally can be calculated from the regression equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of ultrasonic irradiation on the viscosity of oil sample
ce:section.ce:para: (1) The input power of the ultrasonic transducer is 50 W

The experimental results obtained when the input power of the ultrasonic transducer is 50 W are shown in Fig. 6 , where Fig. 6(a) indicates the changes of viscosity and temperature of the oil sample with the ultrasonic irradiation time, and the blue, green and red spheres represent the measured viscosity of the oil sample under ultrasonic irradiation, the theoretical viscosity calculated by fitting the model, and the real-time temperature variation, respectively.

Fig. 6(b) shows the variation of viscosity reduction rate (VRR)of the oil sample with time within 30 mins, and the blue and green pentagrams express the actual viscosity change rate by ultrasonic irradiation and the effect of temperature on viscosity calculated by the model, separately. The VRR is calculated as follows:
Viscosity Reduction Rate
=
μ 0
- μ
μ 0
∗ 100 %
where
μ 0
is the initial viscosity of the oil sample, and μ indicates the viscosity of the oil sample under different conditions.

Overall, the viscosity of the oil sample gradually decreased with the increase of ultrasonic irradiation time, and its internal temperature did not change much in the first five minutes, and then gradually increased to about 45 °C. For the results obtained by regression equation and oil sample temperature calculation considering only the effect of temperature change on the viscosity of the oil sample, the viscosity gradually decreases with the increase of temperature. It is obviously shown from the blue and green curves that the result of ultrasonic irradiation is not the same as the effect of simple warming on the viscosity of the oil sample, which indicates that there are other effects of ultrasonic irradiation besides acting on the oil sample through thermal effect, and the intrinsic mechanism will be discussed in detail in section 3.3.

In addition, in the first five minutes, the temperature of the oil sample does not exhibit an obvious change, and the viscosity of the oil sample decreases by about 10% at this time, indicating that the ultrasonic irradiation has a more obvious promotion effect on the reduction of the viscosity of the oil sample through other ways besides the thermal effect. After five minutes, the viscosity of the oil sample still continues to decrease, but only the effect of warming will be slightly stronger, indicating that the overall effect of ultrasonic irradiation will slightly inhibit the change of the viscosity of the oil sample at that time. After about 27 mins, the effect of the two ways on the viscosity will gradually be equal.

From Fig. 6(b), it can be seen that the VRR of the oil sample gradually increases with the growth of ultrasonic irradiation time, and the growth rate is faster in the first 27 min, then slowly increases, and reaches the maximum of 60.4% at 30 mins The effect of ultrasonic irradiation on VRR is stronger than that of temperature alone in the first five minutes, and then the degree of effect slightly decreases, but the VRR under both conditions approaches the same value at about 27 mins.
(2) The input power of the ultrasonic transducer is 100 W

The viscosity and temperature variation results obtained when the input electrical power of the ultrasonic transducer is 100 W are shown in Fig. 7 , where the contents represented by the graphs (a) and (b) and the different color curves are the same as those represented under the condition of 50 W.

Similarly, the viscosity of the oil sample gradually decreases with the increase of the ultrasonic irradiation time. However, compared with the electric power of 50 W, the temperature of the oil sample rise faster and higher, reaching 66.7 °C at 30 mins, and the VRR reaches about 80%. It is obvious that the ultrasonic effect is more intense at this time, and the influence on viscosity is greater.

In the first five minutes, the temperature of the oil sample also does not change considerably, but the VRR also reaches about 10% at this time, indicating that the ultrasonic irradiation does not affect the viscosity of the oil sample through the thermal effect at this time. There is also an inflection point near five minutes, and after the inflection point, the temperature increase alone would reduce the viscosity of the oil sample more compared with the ultrasonic irradiation. And around 25 mins, the rate of viscosity variation decreases and the effect of both conditions on the viscosity of the oil sample tends to be the same, which is earlier than the results at 50 W of electric power.

The variation of the VRR is likewise greater from the effect of ultrasound at the beginning to the higher effect of warming on viscosity later, and finally, the effect of both tends to be the same, where the time of tending to be the same is also earlier than the result at 50 W of electric power.
(3) The input power of the ultrasonic transducer is 150 W

The results of the variations of viscosity, temperature, and VRR of the oil sample, when the input electric power of the ultrasonic transducer is 150 W, are shown in Fig. 8 , where the contents represented by Fig. 8(a) and (b) and different color curves are the same as those expressed in the first two experimental conditions.

Similar to the results of the first two experimental conditions, the viscosity of the oil sample gradually decreases with the increase of the ultrasonic irradiation time, with the decreasing rate from fast to slow. The actual viscosity value of the oil sample compared with the result calculated by the regression equation still have an inflection point around 5 mins, and the jumping point of the temperature of the oil sample in the first five minutes may be caused by the unstable work of the transducer, it leads to the local temperature inside the oil sample to a sudden rise in temperature in 3–4 min. After 4 min, the working state of the transducer tends to be stable, and the rules of temperature and viscosity change to be consistent with that at 50 W and 100 W. And it also reaches a maximum of about 90 °C at 30 mins. In addition, the electric power is 150 W when the effect of the two conditions on the viscosity of the approximate effect of the time is significantly earlier to about 20 mins, and the influence of ultrasonic irradiation tends to become greater.

The VRR also increases gradually with the growth of time, and the growth rate increases slowly from fast to the maximum, which is about 91.3%. After 25 mins, the effect of ultrasound on the VRR has a tendency to increase compared with only warming.

In summary, under the three electric power conditions, the viscosity of the oil sample gradually decreases with the increase of ultrasonic irradiation time, and its temperature also gradually increases. The comparison between the actual viscosity and the viscosity of the oil sample when only warming up, both appear inflection point of nearly 5 mins. Before 5 mins, the temperature of the oil sample does not change much, the viscosity of the ultrasonic irradiation of the oil sample is reduced by about 10% at this moment. After 5 mins, the viscosity corresponding to the two conditions continues to reduce, but only warming up would have more influence on the viscosity. And sometimes after 20 mins, the viscosity change under the two actions would tend to be the same again. Besides, under the same ultrasonic irradiation time condition, the increase of electric power obviously makes the temperature of the oil sample becomes higher and also increases the VRR, while the time of convergence between experimental and fitted data would be the same.

While measuring the real-time changes of oil sample viscosity and temperature, we also use a hydrophone to receive the noise signal formed by the ultrasonic transducer in the liquid and visualize the signal observation and acquisition by an oscilloscope, then finally post-process the data using MATLAB and Origin software.

The noise signal could determine whether cavitation effects existed in addition to thermal effects during the ultrasonic irradiation and the effect of cavitation strength on viscosity. Because the collected data are too many to present one by one, the data of the first five minutes of ultrasonic irradiation at an electric power of 50 W are presented as an example.

Fig. 9 shows the time-domain waveforms for the first 5 min of ultrasonic irradiation at an electrical power of 50 W. The ten sets of data are plotted uniformly in a waterfall plot, with different colors indicating the time-domain signals at different moments. Because this paper only compares relative magnitudes, the voltage data collected by the oscilloscope is not converted to sound pressure, and the figure shows the result of normalizing the voltage. The frequency domain waveform obtained after doing Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) on the time domain signal is shown in Fig. 10 .

Each group of frequency domain signals in Fig. 10 corresponds to the time domain signals in Fig. 9. Since the center frequency of the transducer is around 20.7 kHz, the changes in fundamental frequency, subharmonic frequency, superharmonic frequency, and second harmonic are mainly compared, i.e., the frequency domain signals from 0 to 40.2 kHz are intercepted for comparison. The peaks of different signals indicate the normalized amplitude results obtained after the FFT of the time domain signal, and the different colors in the figure indicate the changes in the frequency domain signal at different moments.

From the results in the figure, it can be seen that there is a cavitation effect in the process of ultrasonic irradiation on the oil sample, but the peak of the spectral line only at the fundamental frequency and octave frequency is more obvious, so the strength of cavitation could not be judged by the relative height of the spectral line at the four frequencies. The peaks at the subharmonic and superharmonic frequencies are weaker, probably due to that the broadband noise is stronger and drowns out the spectral lines at these two frequencies. Therefore, we can get the effect of the strength of cavitation indirectly by calculating the integrated area of the broadband noise with respect to the frequency axis through the size of the integrated area [35,36].

Ten points around the fundamental frequency and octave frequency are removed, and the integrated area of the spectral lines under different conditions in the range of 0–40.2 kHz is calculated, and the normalized comparison results of the area at different ultrasonic irradiation times under different powers are obtained as shown in Fig. 11 .

The calculated results at three electrical powers are plotted in the form of a three-dimensional waterfall diagram, where green, blue, and red mean the situation when the electrical power is 50, 100, and 150 W, respectively.

As is shown in the figure, with the increase of ultrasonic irradiation time, the integral area increases in the later stage of ultrasonic irradiation than in the earlier stage on the whole, which indicates that the cavitation effect is gradually enhanced. In addition, there are some changes in the integral area near the inflection point of viscosity change of 5 mins, especially after 20 mins, the integral area would increase significantly. The integration area is also somewhat larger for the other two conditions compared to the case of electric power of 50 W, both of which indicate the enhancement of the cavitation effect.

However, the large fluctuation of the curve at 150 W may be caused that the ultrasonic generation system used in this paper cannot realize the automatic tracking of the resonant frequency. With the increase of time, the working state of the transducer has changed (including the change of resonant frequency and load power). At this time, it is necessary to manually adjust the frequency to increase the load power, so that the transducer could reach the best working state. Therefore, the integral area does not increase as a whole as in the other two cases, but decreases first and then increases. This phenomenon can be interpreted by that the load power decreases due to frequency imbalance, resulting in the reduction of the integral area. Later, when the transducer reaches the resonant frequency, the load power gradually increases. With the increase of time, the internal temperature of the oil sample gradually increases, and the integral area also gradually increases. The final integral area at 150 W is larger than the other two cases, indicating that the cavitation intensity at 150 W is stronger.
The next section will focus on the relationship between the viscosity variation law and the thermal effect and cavitation intensity in section 3.3.

The temperature of the oil sample increases during the ultrasonic irradiation, and the higher the electric power, the faster the temperature of the oil sample increases and the larger the final peak, but the results of the regression equation between the real-time temperature change data and the viscosity-temperature curve of the oil sample show that the ultrasonic irradiation does not affect the viscosity through the thermal effect alone [5,37–41]. At the same time, we confirm the existence of the cavitation effect in the process of ultrasonic irradiation by cavitation noise method and also observe the existence of certain fluidity in the oil sample during the experiment, which also indicates that the mechanical stirring effect also exists. Therefore, this paper confirms the existence of the cavitation effect, thermal effect, and mechanical effect in the process of ultrasonic irradiation on oil samples, and then focuses on the analysis and discussion of the mechanism of real-time change of oil sample viscosity through these three effects.

The ultrasonic effect on the oil sample has part of the acoustic energy into heat, cavitation effect also accompanies by local high temperature and high-pressure phenomenon, while the mechanical action would exist frictional heat, these parts of the heat together are called thermal effect on the oil sample. And the viscous temperature property of the oil sample is relatively obvious, which is one of the reasons for the viscosity with the growth of ultrasonic irradiation time and reduction.

As we have mentioned before, the actual viscosity change is not exactly the same as the result calculated from the regression equation, indicating that cavitation and mechanical effects also play a crucial role in the viscosity change of the oil sample. The mechanical effect is to make the ultrasound act more uniformly on different parts of the oil sample by stirring, and the cavitation effect is accompanied by a huge energy release, which will have a great influence on the internal structure and chemical components of the oil sample, so we think this is one of the important factors that lead to the large difference between the measured viscosity and the results calculated by the regression equation.

Under the three electric power conditions, the temperature of the oil sample does not change much in the first five minutes of ultrasonic irradiation, and the VRR reaches about 10% at this time, indicating that the cavitation effect on the viscosity at this time occupies the dominant role, which increases the content of light components in the oil sample by destroying the internal molecular structure of the oil sample and breaking the carbon chains, thus reducing the viscosity of the oil sample [1,42,43]. At about 5–20 mins of ultrasonic irradiation, the viscosity of the oil sample continues to decrease, but the VRR is slightly lower compared with the viscosity change when only warming up, indicating that the cavitation effect will play a certain inhibitory effect on the viscosity change at this time, and the destroyed molecular structure of the oil sample may be reorganized during this time, which leads to the reduction of lighter components, so the viscosity change will be slightly weakened. After 20 mins, the viscosity changes under both conditions are gradually the same, and at an electric power of 150 W, the effect of sonication on viscosity even tends to exceed that of pure temperature increase, indicating that sonication causes the previously reconstituted carbon chains to be broken, causing the content of lighter components to become more abundant and therefore more influential than a single temperature change.

In other words, with the increase of electric power, the cavitation effect will be enhanced gradually, and the temperature will rise more, which will cause the VRR of the oil sample to change more. This shows that although the viscosity change of the oil sample will be slightly lower than that of the pure temperature increase in a certain period of time of ultrasonic irradiation, the overall effect of ultrasonic irradiation on the oil sample is multiple, such as it will destroy the internal molecular structure of the oil sample, produce a stronger stirring effect to promote the flow of liquid, etc. Therefore, ultrasonic irradiation under different conditions can be selected according to different requirements to achieve better results.

In this work, the transducer with larger energy output is developed independently, the acoustic field distribution in the experimental device is optimized by finite element theory and orthogonal experimental method, the real-time change of viscosity of oil sample with ultrasonic irradiation time is investigated, and the mechanism of viscosity change of oil sample is analyzed by using temperature recorder and cavitation noise method, and the following conclusions are mainly obtained:
(1)
The greatest influence on the sound pressure in the reaction chamber is in the height Z direction, followed by the width X direction, and finally the depth Y direction. Furthermore, the optimal sound field conditions in the reaction cavity are 25 mm in the X-direction, 15 mm in the Y-direction, and 45 mm in the Z-direction.
(2)
The viscosity of the oil sample varies exponentially with the increase in temperature, and the regression equation of viscosity and temperature dependence is obtained by fitting.
(3)
With the increase of ultrasonic irradiation time, the viscosity of the oil sample will gradually decrease and its temperature will gradually increase. The effect of ultrasonic irradiation on the viscosity of the oil sample compared with the viscosity change when heating only, both show an inflection point near 5 mins. Before 5 mins, the temperature of the oil sample does not change much, at which time the viscosity of the oil sample under ultrasonic irradiation decreases by about 10%. After 5 mins, the viscosity corresponding to the two conditions continues to decrease, but the effect of heating only on the viscosity is greater. Sometime after 20 mins, the viscosity change under both conditions will be identical again.
(4)
Under the same conditions of ultrasonic irradiation time, the increase of electric power will obviously make the temperature of the oil sample will become higher, and also increase the VRR of the oil sample. In addition, With the increase of ultrasonic irradiation time, the cavitation effect is gradually enhanced, and the cavitation intensity also changes after 5 and 20 mins, and the cavitation effect is also enhanced with the increase of electric power.
(5)
The ultrasonic irradiation does not affect the viscosity of the oil sample solely through the thermal effect. The existence of the cavitation effect during the ultrasonic irradiation was confirmed by the cavitation noise method, and it was observed that the mechanical stirring effect in the liquid also exists at the same time, and the ultrasound acts on the liquid through these three effects together and affects its internal structure and components, ultimately changing its viscosity.

In addition, to further reveal the real-time change mechanism of viscosity, it is also necessary to analyze the variations of chemical composition and structure of oil samples in real-time and the relationship between cavitation and viscosity change by combining high-speed photography and bubble dynamics theory [44].

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11904384) and the Key Research Program of Frontier Sciences, CAS (No. ZDBS-LY-SLH037). Thanks go to Dr. Feida Cai (Institute of Acoustics, CAS) for the inspiring discussion about the experimental scheme, and Bin Xu senior engineer (Beijing Chang Cheng Aeronautical Measurement and Control Technology Co., Ltd), and Yulin Gong (Hydramotion, Ltd) for help in using the instrument. In addition, I would like to express my gratitude to Dr. Mi Zhang (Chongqing University of Science and Technology), Guanwen Sun, and Qin Chang for their assistance in the simulation, experiment, and data processing. Also, thanks go to my classmate Yuchen Zang for help with the English corrections.
